detail: field='cause.mode' substring='device shuts off suddenly during use with an audible click' not in quote

original_draft.source_quote: One of the batteries may show "reverse voltage" while others are well below "critical". Measure each of the 8 cells individually with a DMM; use a 90 W charger and attempt to bring cells back to 3.4 V per cell.

evidence.source_quote_substring: One of the batteries may show "reverse voltage" while others are well below "critical"
evidence.reasoning: J1 is the battery connector that links the 18650 cells to the motherboard. A reverse-voltage or critically-low cell effectively presents an open or collapsed supply to the board through J1.

evidence.source_quote_substring: device shuts off suddenly during use with an audible click
evidence.reasoning: The supply path through J1 opens (protection circuitry trips), cutting battery power — matching 'open' mode.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: One or more LiFePO4 cells deep-discharge to reverse polarity; the battery protection circuitry trips and the J1 connector path opens, cutting battery supply rails BAT through BAT8 and causing sudden shutdown.

original_draft.source_quote: When the charge board is dead, other community posts describe connecting pins 2 and 3 on the J1 plug to bypass it temporarily and allow firmware reflashing while a replacement board is sourced.

evidence.source_quote_substring: connecting pins 2 and 3 on the J1 plug to bypass it temporarily
evidence.reasoning: J1 is the explicit refdes in both the registry and the graph. The charge board is dead and J1 is the interface between it and the motherboard.

evidence.source_quote_substring: When the charge board is dead
evidence.reasoning: The charge board (and by extension the J1 supply path) is completely dead — no charging or battery power delivered through this connector.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: The battery/charge board PCB fails completely. No charging or battery power is delivered through J1. The BAT and CHGBAT rails collapse. The machine cannot run on battery or charge cells. A temporary workaround bridges J1 pins 2-3.

detail: field='cause.refdes' substring='Faulty or misseated eDP/DSI flat cable between the motherboard and the display panel' not in quote

original_draft.source_quote: After reassembly the screen did not turn on; HDMI works fine; the screen flickers into existence for a few minutes then disappears — and this happens without anything touching the connector.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Faulty or misseated eDP/DSI flat cable between the motherboard and the display panel
evidence.reasoning: The eDP/DSI flat cable is the component identified as the likely cause of the internal display failure. It exists in the registry as 'eDP / DSI flat cable'.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the screen flickers into existence for a few minutes then disappears — and this happens without anything touching the connector
evidence.reasoning: Intermittent open connection in the FFC ribbon cable — contact opens and the display goes dark.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: The eDP/DSI flat cable is misseated or damaged after reassembly. The internal display does not turn on (or flickers then dies) while external HDMI continues to work normally. The EDP_BL_VCC and EDP_LCD_VCC rails may appear dead from the panel's perspective due to the open cable.

original_draft.source_quote: A prior report was incorrectly attributed to a detached port but turned out to be a faulty cable; a later confirmed case involved the port actually detaching after upgrading to a newer motherboard + sound board. Visual inspection and gentle tug test on the connector shell.

evidence.source_quote_substring: a later confirmed case involved the port actually detaching after upgrading to a newer motherboard + sound board
evidence.reasoning: The micro-HDMI port physically detaches from the PCB — the connection opens. The refdes 'micro-HDMI port' exists in the registry.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the port actually detaching
evidence.reasoning: Physical detachment of the connector from PCB pads creates an open circuit for all HDMI signals and power.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: The micro-HDMI port mechanically detaches from the motherboard PCB pads under cable stress or during reassembly. External monitor output goes black. This is a local/mechanical open failure with no cascade to other power rails or components.

original_draft.source_quote: When the power adapter is plugged in, the two white LEDs and green LED on the compute module are present; the LPC serial is interactive; new batteries report healthy voltages; all LPC commands work — but entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off and nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1. Inspect the SODIMM slot contacts for debris or damage.

evidence.source_quote_substring: entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off and nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1
evidence.reasoning: The CPU SOM (SODIMM module / compute module) does not respond to the power-on command — it is dead or not receiving its enable signal. CPU SOM is in the registry.

evidence.source_quote_substring: nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1
evidence.reasoning: The SOM is completely unresponsive after receiving the power-on command — dead mode. LPC is healthy but SOM produces no output.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: The CPU SOM fails to boot after the LPC issues the '1P' power-on command. The LPC is interactive and all status indicators look healthy, but the SOM produces no serial output and the main LCD stays blank. This is a local SOM failure (possibly damaged SODIMM contacts or a failed module).

original_draft.source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Verify cell voltages individually; reflash LPC firmware via a second computer.

evidence.source_quote_substring: It stopped working: it was not turning on
evidence.reasoning: U14 is the source of the LPC_VCC rail in the graph. A regulating-low condition on LPC_VCC would explain why the LPC cannot initialise and D9 stays dark.

evidence.source_quote_substring: when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark
evidence.reasoning: If LPC_VCC is present but below threshold, the LPC cannot boot but some indication remains — matching a regulating_low scenario rather than a fully dead rail.

evidence.source_quote_substring: It stopped working: it was not turning on
evidence.reasoning: A low LPC_VCC would cause the LPC controller to fail to initialise, leaving downstream dependent rails dead.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: U14 (LPC_VCC regulator) outputs a voltage below threshold, preventing the LPC controller from booting. D9 stays dark as the LPC cannot assert the power-OK signal. LPC_VCC is nominally present but too low to sustain LPC operation.

original_draft.source_quote: After a period of perfect suspend/resume cycles the LPC will not wake up the board; the LCD shows "Waking up LPC… 0%" and goes blank after a short time (~400 ms). Cold-cycling battery power resolves it temporarily.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the LPC will not wake up the board
evidence.reasoning: The LPC controller fails to resume, matching the BT1 system controller refdes in the graph. The failure is a dead/unresponsive state after suspend.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the LCD shows "Waking up LPC… 0%" and goes blank after a short time (~400 ms)
evidence.reasoning: The controller is completely unresponsive — dead mode — as it cannot complete wake-up sequence and the LCD gets no response.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: After many suspend/resume cycles, the LPC firmware fails to resume and the board cannot wake. Cold-cycling battery power is the only recovery. This is a local firmware failure with no cascading rail loss — rails may still be present but LPC is unresponsive.

original_draft.source_quote: One USB port was not functioning properly; subsequently the kernel log filled at high speed with `reform2_lpc spi1.0: lpc_command: c/0 checksum mismatch: af expected, 0 received`. Reflash LPC firmware and check for LPC firmware/driver version mismatch.

evidence.source_quote_substring: reform2_lpc spi1.0: lpc_command: c/0 checksum mismatch: af expected, 0 received
evidence.reasoning: The LPC controller (mapped to BT1 in graph context as the system controller) is the source of the SPI checksum mismatch — it is not responding correctly to SPI transactions, indicating a dead/unresponsive firmware state.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Reflash LPC firmware and check for LPC firmware/driver version mismatch
evidence.reasoning: The LPC is unresponsive to SPI commands (returning 0 instead of valid checksums), which maps to a dead mode for the controller.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: The LPC firmware becomes unresponsive on the SPI bus (LPC_SPI_BUS / spi1.0), causing the kernel driver to flood the log with checksum mismatch errors. Battery status becomes unavailable. This is a local firmware failure with no cascading power rail loss.

original_draft.source_quote: Firmware updates greatly reduce battery drain in "off" mode by making use of deep sleep modes on both the keyboard and the LPC controller. Check installed LPC and keyboard firmware versions before probing hardware.

evidence.source_quote_substring: battery drain in "off" mode
evidence.reasoning: Excessive quiescent current through J1 (the battery interface) drains the cells even when the machine is off — modelled as a leaky short on the battery supply path.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Firmware updates greatly reduce battery drain in "off" mode
evidence.reasoning: The high off-state current draw is a resistive leakage path from the battery — leaky_short. The source confirms a non-zero but measurable drain.

evidence.source_quote_substring: battery drain in "off" mode by making use of deep sleep modes on both the keyboard and the LPC controller
evidence.reasoning: Quiescent current in off mode without deep sleep is in the tens of mA range, corresponding to a few hundred ohm equivalent leakage from the battery bus — 500 Ω selected as upper bound of typical range.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: Without firmware deep-sleep support, the LPC controller and keyboard MCU maintain a high quiescent current draw through the J1 battery connector. This slowly drains the LiFePO4 cells even while the device is nominally off, potentially causing cell damage over time.
